So this week we're going to talk about a different kind of statistical model called logistic regression. So you know, last week was a little interlude going back into programming, into SQL. This week we're back into kind of the more mathy side of the class, thinking about prediction modeling.

So, announcements, Lab 9 is due tonight. Homework 7 is due Friday. Next week we'll start on Project 2. If you find this material challenging, which you might because it is, and you want more content review, you can always come to catch up. Sections every Friday from 3:00 to 4:00 in Evans B6. There's a link there if you have not already filled out, please just like take a picture of it, make sure you fill it out sometime ASAP just so that we can schedule everybody and make sure we have all the logistics hired up. There's an Ed post with more info.

Next week is the start of course evaluations. So you know, you'll get a link, you'll be asked to evaluate all the courses that you're taking. I want to say a few words about course evaluations just like in general. And in particular, I want to talk about a study from 2019 that was done at a large public university and they took four classes for like big classes. I think it was like bio.
Political science class, a chemistry class, and two of them.

Add mail instructors to them had female instructors, both were very, all four were pretty experienced instructors and they wanted to understand what the effect is of unconscious bias on student evaluations. And so within each course they did a randomized experiment. Half the students got a standard evaluation, you know, just the usual like fill out your evaluations. The other half got a standard evaluation, but they had a little statement at the top that students could read about unconscious bias and the statement.
Something like this. And I don't want to like, you know, read the whole thing to you now.
But I want to emphasize that it's really about unconscious bias, right? It's talking about how.

Women instructors of color tend to be systematically rated lower in teaching evaluations and white men, even when there are no actual differences in the instruction. So this is a, you know, fact based on evidence that has been shown in many other studies. So they put this paragraph in front of students, and what they found was a pretty significant difference between students who saw the statement and students who didn't.
So we can think of this as a randomized experiment with a control group of the standard evaluation and a treatment group of the standard evaluation, plus this unconscious bias statement.
So what happened?

So for the male instructors, this is what the difference in evals look like. So in blue on the left side of each number we have the control and in red on the right side we have the treatment. And you can see that there is definitely some change. It ended up not being that large, you know, could be explained by chance, but you can see that there is some change, some small change, but no significant differences.
But if you look at the results for the female instructors, again, same control and treatment. I'm not sure why this is Sanford. This should be standard.
This is not a Stanford. This is a large public university, not Berkeley. But what happened with the female instructors was.

A pretty dramatic shift. You can see that in the controls, way more students gave them one twos and threes. Those basically disappeared. One student saw this statement right, and there are a lot more fours and fives.
Now, what can we conclude from this study?
Right. Can we conclude that?

There's unconscious bias. Does that that unconscious bias affect student evaluations? Well, maybe that's possible, but the study doesn't show that decisively, right? It's it's possible that that had a strong effect on student evaluations. But what we can conclude with absolute certainty is that student evaluations are fickle. In other words, they're easy to influence. Maybe you read the statement before. In some cases, maybe you're hungry and you didn't have a, you know, didn't happen to eat breakfast that day.

Maybe it's raining, maybe you're tired. So there are a lot of different factors that can influence evaluations and I want to encourage all of you to keep that in mind as you go through taking evaluations starting next week.

So it's pretty concerning, right? Because they're, for a lot of people, evaluations influence whether or not they get hired, whether or not they get promoted, you know, whether they're up for raises and so on. So it affects people's career progression, it affects people's, you know, lives. So how can we combat this?

Awareness, right So First off, if you are aware that this might be happening with you and it will because you're human, it would happen it happens with me when I take surveys too. It happens to all of us but being aware of what biases might be affecting your judgment and also just like thinking big picture, right when you go to evaluate don't think like, oh, last lecture I saw was like, well, it was like really good so I'm going to rate this person higher. The last texture was like really bad so I'm going to rate them low think big picture, think about how the entire course is run think about.
The You know the entirety of that instructor's teaching.

And complaining is good, but make your complaints constructive, right? As instructors, we love to see feedback that we can act on, right? Saying that like, oh, this person was terrible, I didn't like them is not helpful. But saying that I didn't like X and I would have rather seen Y is a lot more helpful. Because if we see half the students in the class saying that, that tells us, hey, this is something we ought to explore. So be specific, Be constructive in your feedback.
OK. That's all I have to say on that.

OK. So today I'm going to talk about regression versus classification. So we're going to move from regression, which is what we've been doing now so far, to thinking about classification right from instead of predicting continuous stuff, classifying things as like yes or no, stay or go, this or that.

So, you know, we've spent most of our time in this class on linear regression, but there's really a lot of different problems in the world of machine learning. There's many different ways to slice it, but one common way is to say that there's really three big areas. There's supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning.

So supervised learning is like you have some X's and you have some YS and you want to predict Y from X, right? And that's what we've sent most of our time on so far. And specifically, we've spent most of our time in the case where Y is a continuous quantitative variable that we're trying to predict, right? And there's a lot of different methods for doing that. We've focused on one of the most important and fundamental linear regression, but there's really a lot more.

Today and this week, we're going to switch gears to a different kind of supervised learning where we're still in this like we have XS and YS and we want to predict the YS from the XS. But now our YS are going to be categorical and we're going to call these methods classification, right? Instead of predicting A continuous thing, we're going to predict something that is categorical, you know, binary and so on.

Next week we're going to switch over to unsupervised learning, talking about what do you do when it's not like trying to predict Y from X. It's just you have some data and you want to like understand the patterns in it, what's going on without necessarily having some prediction task.

There's also reinforcement learning, which is this idea of you like make a decision and then you get a reward. This is common in game playing. This is a key component of how large language models are trained. This is beyond the scope of this class.

This week I'm going to talk about classification. Next week we're going to talk about unsupervised learning and to learn more about reinforcement learning. It's not covered in this class. I encourage you to take CS188 Data 102. There's plenty of other courses that cover this in a little more detail.
OK, so regression.
Again, the idea with regression is we use quantitative features to predict a numeric unbounded output. In other words, our output can be anything from negative Infinity to a positive Infinity.

So, you know, we've seen stuff like, oh, we want to predict the tip amount from the total bill or we want to predict the mileage of gas mileage of a car from the horsepower. Maybe we want to predict the field goal percent difference between two teams in in the NBA from turnover percent, right. So today we're going to be working a little bit with this data set of NBA data. I think it's from 20/17/2018 season where, you know, we have one row for each game and we have, you know, the team, whether they.
Loss and a bunch of things like the field goal percentage difference. We'll talk about it in a moment.
So this is regression. We want to predict quantitative things that are unbounded.
In classification on the other hand.
Our goal is to predict a categorical or qualitative variable.

So examples of this might be in that TIPS data set. Maybe instead of predicting the amount, we want to predict what day of the week it is, right? Does this bill, this look like a Wednesday? Does it look like a Thursday? Does it look like a Saturday? We want to do these kind of categorical predictions or what model of car are we looking at or even what make right different kinds of cars from the horsepower or predict which team won, right? Did my team win or did they lose?
So these are, this is kind of a fundamentally different kind of problem because instead of justice like predicting a number, we have to predict a specific categorical variable.

So the way we're going to go about this is we're going to model probability for each of the classes. We're going to call each of these like categories, classes, right? So in the day of the week example, the classes are Monday through Sunday.

And the car example, the classes are a different car manufacturers and in the, you know, wind prediction, the classes are yes, no. So we're going to model probabilities for each class and we're going to take those probabilities and use them to make a decision.

Right. So we calculate a probability and a little bit of a spoiler of how we're going to do it is we're going to do something similar to linear regression, right? You should see this and you should think, aha, that looks like linear regression. But then we have this like thing Sigma in front of it. What does that mean? We'll get to that.

So this is how we calculate these probabilities. And then we have some kind of decision rule that says, OK, I have a probability. You know, I think this Bill, this game, there's a like 60% chance we won. And you come up with some threshold, right? Maybe I say, well, if my prediction is more than 70%, then I say yes. Otherwise I say no, right? More than 70% is a win. Less than 70% is a prediction of loss.
Again, how do you decide on that threshold? We'll talk about that on Thursday.
And then at the end we come up with a variable that says, you know, whether they win one or lost 0.

So there's different kinds of classification. The simplest is binary classification, just like a zero or one. And this is where we're going to spend our time this week in DATA 100. And this is often where classes spend the most time because you learn most of the fundamental ideas here and it's pretty easy to generalize from them once you see the more advanced stuff.
So responses are Y variables are always going to be either zero or one.

So we have some flexibility in how we decide what A1 means and what a 0 means, right? If you're thinking about, you know, like a basketball game, you can encode one or zeros, either win or loss or loss or win. But like, in general, it's more intuitive to say that a win is A1 and a loss is a 0, right? So like, yes, you can do it arbitrarily, but usually there's like a convention or there's like the thing that makes the most sense. So keep that in mind.
You know, one or zero is arbitrary. Usually there's a convention, sometimes there's not, in which case it's arbitrary.
Right. So there's like win or lose, you know, does somebody have a disease or not? Or is this e-mail a spam e-mail or a non spam which we sometimes call ham e-mail?
And this example here is actually what you'll be doing in Project B starting next week.

Beyond binary classification, there's multiclass. So thinking where we have not just two labels, but many, right? Maybe you're categorizing animals as belonging to one of several species. Examples come from like image labeling and annotation. There's a lot of different examples of this. And there's even structured prediction tasks, right, where we want to predict instead of like a discrete class, we're predicting some kind of object, maybe a word or like a phrase.
So you know machine translation large language model.
Fall into this category.
In this class, we're going to be focusing primarily on binary classification. I encourage you to take other classes like CS 189 and so on to learn more about different kinds of methods.
OK, so how does modeling work?

Again, in this class, most of the time that's going to be squared loss or L2 loss. Then we fit the model. What is fitting the model? It means choosing the values of the coefficients.
That are the best possible ones. Best in the sense of lowest loss? How do we do that?

Well, we use gradient descent, right? And in practice we use scikit learn, which takes care of the gradient descent for us. We might want to use regularization, but you know, the idea is we want to minimize the loss or the average loss. And then we evaluate the model performance. We say how well did our model do? And we do that by looking at the residuals. We can look at R-squared and so on.
So how much of this translates when we go to classification?

But we're going to have to choose a completely different kind of model because linear regression doesn't work, and we're going to have to choose a different loss function. And that's what we're going to spend most of today on.
